author: Augustine of Hippo
book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
Four Moral Duties Regarding the Love of God, of Which Love the Reward is Eternal Life and the Knowledge of the Truth.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Four Moral Duties Regarding the Love of God, of Which Love the Reward is Eternal Life and the Knowledge of the Truth.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: I need say no more about right conduct.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For if God is man’s chief good, which you cannot deny, it clearly follows, since to seek the chief good is to live well, that to live well is nothing else but to love God with all the heart, with all the soul, with all the mind; and, as arising from this, that this love must be preserved entire and incorrupt, which is the part of temperance; that it give way before no troubles, which is the part of fortitude; that it serve no other, which is the part of justice; that it be watchful in its inspection of things lest craft or fraud steal in, which is the part of prudence.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: This is the one perfection of man, by which alone he can succeed in attaining to the purity of truth.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: This both Testaments enjoin in concert; this is commended on both sides alike.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Why do you continue to cast reproaches on Scriptures of which you are ignorant?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Do you not see the folly of your attack upon books which only those who do not understand them find fault with, and which only those who find fault fail in understanding?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For neither can an enemy know them, nor can one who knows them be other than a friend to them.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Let us then, as many as have in view to reach eternal life, love God with all the heart, with all the soul, with all the mind.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: For eternal life contains the whole reward in the promise of which we rejoice; nor can the reward precede desert, nor be given to a man before he is worthy of it.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: What can be more unjust than this, and what is more just than God?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: We should not then demand the reward before we deserve to get it.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Here, perhaps, it is not out of place to ask what is eternal life; or rather let us hear the Bestower of it: "This," He says, "is life eternal, that they should know Thee, the true God, and Jesus Christ whom Thou hast sent."

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: So eternal life is the knowledge of the truth.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: See, then, how perverse and preposterous is the character of those who think that their teaching of the knowledge of God will make us perfect, when this is the reward of those already perfect!

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: What else, then, have we to do but first to love with full affection Him whom we desire to know?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Catholic Church.
verses.text: Hence arises that principle on which we have all along insisted, that there is nothing more wholesome in the Catholic Church than using authority before argument.
